coredata.dc:title: A novel ultrafast synthesis of N, S-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent nanoprobe for entacapone and clonazepam estimation in tablets and human plasma: Compliance with greenness metrics and content uniformity testing
coredata.dc:creator: Magdy, Galal
Aboelkassim, Eman
El-Domany, Ramadan A.
Belal, Fathalla
coredata.dc:description: An ultrafast and green method was proposed for the microwave-assisted synthesis of N, S-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CDs) in only 90 s. The proposed method used thiourea as a source of sulfur and nitrogen and Dill (Anethum vulgaris) as a green carbon source. Characterization of the produced N,S-CDs was performed using several microscopic and spectroscopic methods. They were utilized as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the entacapone (ENT) and clonazepam (CLZ) estimation. After excitation at 310 nm, the developed N,S-CDs revealed an emission band at 380 nm. HR-TEM showed spherical N,S-CDs with a size range of 1–5 nm. The proposed method is the first report on the estimation of each of ENT and CLZ using N,S-CDs and the first one to use Dill as a carbon source for the preparation of N,S-CDs. The prepared N,S-CDs exhibited native fluorescence that was quantitatively quenched by each of ENT and CLZ over the ranges of 1.0-15.0 and 2.0 – 25.0 μM, respectively. The mechanism of N,S-CDs fluorescence quenching was carefully studied. The suggested approach was employed for the determination of ENT and CLZ in their pharmaceutical formulations and human plasma samples with acceptable % RSD and % recoveries (98.50-101.31%). Moreover, the content uniformity testing of the CLZ tablets was efficiently carried out according to the United States Pharmacopoeia recommendations. The excellent greenness and eco-friendliness of the proposed approach were demonstrated using the complementary Green Analytical Procedure Index (ComplexGAPI) and the Analytical GREEnness (AGREE). Validation of the proposed method was performed using ICHQ2 (R1) guidelines.

head.ce:title: A novel ultrafast synthesis of N, S-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent nanoprobe for entacapone and clonazepam estimation in tablets and human plasma: Compliance with greenness metrics and content uniformity testing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An ultrafast and green method was proposed for the microwave-assisted synthesis of N, S-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CDs) in only 90 s. The proposed method used thiourea as a source of sulfur and nitrogen and Dill (Anethum vulgaris) as a green carbon source. Characterization of the produced N,S-CDs was performed using several microscopic and spectroscopic methods. They were utilized as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the entacapone (ENT) and clonazepam (CLZ) estimation. After excitation at 310 nm, the developed N,S-CDs revealed an emission band at 380 nm. HR-TEM showed spherical N,S-CDs with a size range of 1–5 nm. The proposed method is the first report on the estimation of each of ENT and CLZ using N,S-CDs and the first one to use Dill as a carbon source for the preparation of N,S-CDs. The prepared N,S-CDs exhibited native fluorescence that was quantitatively quenched by each of ENT and CLZ over the ranges of 1.0-15.0 and 2.0 – 25.0 μM, respectively. The mechanism of N,S-CDs fluorescence quenching was carefully studied. The suggested approach was employed for the determination of ENT and CLZ in their pharmaceutical formulations and human plasma samples with acceptable % RSD and % recoveries (98.50-101.31%). Moreover, the content uniformity testing of the CLZ tablets was efficiently carried out according to the United States Pharmacopoeia recommendations. The excellent greenness and eco-friendliness of the proposed approach were demonstrated using the complementary Green Analytical Procedure Index (ComplexGAPI) and the Analytical GREEnness (AGREE). Validation of the proposed method was performed using ICHQ2 (R1) guidelines.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Entacapone (ENT) is a monocarboxylic acid amide drug used for the management of Parkinson's disease combined with carbidopa and levodopa as adjunctive therapy. It exerts its action by decreasing the metabolism of carbidopa and levodopa by inhibiting the catechol-O-methyltransferase enzyme peripherally, leading to increasing their bioavailability and duration of action (Schrag, 2005). The IUPAC name of ENT is (E)-2-cyano-3-(3,4-dihydroxy-5-nitrophenyl)-N,N-diethylprop-2-enamide (National Center and for Biotechnology Information) (Fig. 1 a).

Clonazepam (CLZ) is a benzodiazepine drug used for managing seizure and panic disorders (Cloos, 2005; Chouinard, 2006). It has also been used off-label as adjunctive or monotherapy therapy for treating tardive dyskinesia, insomnia, mania, and REM sleep behavior disorder (Curtin and Schulz, 2004; Khouzam, 2015). It exerts its action by acting as a GABA-A receptor agonist, decreasing the excitability of neurons. The IUPAC name of CLZ is 5-(2-chlorophenyl)-7-nitro-1,3-dihydro-1,4-benzodiazepin-2-one (National Center and for Biotechnology Information) (Fig. 1b).

Recently, a variety of analytical techniques, including, spectrofluorimetry (Belal et al., 2017a; Ahmadi et al., 2019), luminescence (Min et al., 2021), spectrophotometry (Koradia et al., 2009; Chalikwar et al., 2012), electrochemical (Aftab et al., 2019; Naghian et al., 2021; Budak et al., 2023), various chromatographic techniques (Ramakrishna et al., 2005; Burmaoğlu and Sağlık Aslan, 2020; Soukhova et al., 2011), and capillary electrophoresis (Zeid et al., 2016) were used for ENT determination in its dosage forms and biological fluids. Similarly, for CLZ, analytical techniques including, spectrofluorimetry (Ghafarloo et al., 2020; Ibrahim et al., 2016; Belal et al., 2017b), chemiluminescence (Chaichi and Alijanpour, 2014), spectrophotometry (Kakde and Satone, 2009; Hadi, 2015; Salem et al., 2004), electrochemistry (Rizk et al., 2018; Sachdeva et al., 2021), and chromatographic techniques (Chèze et al., 2004; Bhimanadhuni et al., 2012; Acikkol et al., 2009) were also used.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of nanomaterials with a size range from 2 to 10 nm. Recently, CDs have gained a lot of interest owing to their excellent biocompatibility, simple synthesis, high photostability, and low cost. Many researchers have focused on improving the chemical, electrical, and optical properties of CDs by surface functionalization or doping with hetero-atoms (Somaraj et al., 2022; Magdy et al., 2023a).

Synthesis of CDs could be categorized as follows: “bottom-up” and “top-down”. In “top-down” approach, CDs are synthesized through the breakdown of larger carbon structures into smaller carbon nanoparticles, which are usually performed by different chemical and physical techniques as chemical electrochemical oxidation and laser ablation. On the other hand, in the “bottom-up” method, CDs are synthesized by making up smaller carbon materials into carbon nanoparticles utilizing chemical methods as hydrothermal, pyrolysis, ultrasonication, and microwave treatment (Magdy et al., 2023a; AlSalem et al., 2023; Al- et al., 2023). Compared to other CDs synthetic methods, microwave synthesis is an efficient source of heating, increasing reaction rates and speeding up synthesis. It ensures homogeneous heating and improved control over the process parameters. It is also considered the most straightforward, simple, and rapid technique used for the synthesis of CDs with high quality and quantum yield (Magdy et al., 2023a).

The doping of CDs can be divided based on the number of doped heteroatoms into single-atom or multi-atom doping, which is more favored owing to the synergistic effect between the doping heteroatoms creating unique electronic structures (Miao et al., 2020; Magdy et al., 2022a, 2023b; Alossaimi et al., 2023a, 2023b).

Nitrogen and sulfur are the most used hetero atoms for doping carbon quantum dots. They could produce extra active sites, new defects sites, and electron pairs, altering the chemical activities of the carbon nanoparticles since they have similar electronegativity (C:2.55, S:2.58), strong valence bond, and atomic size close to that of carbon (N:0.75, C:0.77) (Somaraj et al., 2022; Belal et al., 2023). Doped CDs can be used in many applications, including, sensors, catalysis, drug delivery, and bioimaging (Somaraj et al., 2022; Magdy et al., 2023a; Chen et al., 2015; Abdel-Lateef et al., 2023).

The current work provides a green one-step approach for the N,S-CDs' synthesis utilizing a domestic microwave. The synthesis of green N,S-CDs was carried out in only 90 s using thiourea as a source of nitrogen and sulfur and Dill (Anethum vulgaris) as a natural source of carbon. The proposed method is the first analytical report on the N,S-CDs usage for the ENT and CLZ estimation. It is also the first one on the usage of Dill as a source of carbon. Characterization of the prepared N,S-CDs was carried out, and the mechanism of N,S-CDs fluorescence quenching by each of ENT and CLZ was additionally investigated. The suggested approach was efficiently applied for ENT and CLZ estimation in their pharmaceutical formulations and human plasma samples with acceptable %RSD and high % recoveries. In order to increase the proposed method's accuracy, testing of content uniformity was performed as well, following the United States Pharmacopoeia (USP) requirements (Rockville, 2007). One of the most crucial criteria to assess the batch's quality in a therapeutic product specification is content uniformity (Magdy et al., 2022b). Testing for content uniformity makes sure patients receive the proper dosage and verifies that a drug's strength stays within the defined acceptable ranges (Vranić and Uzunović, 2008). Moreover, the greenness profile of the suggested approach was considered utilizing the complementary Green Analytical Procedure Index (ComplexGAPI) and the Analytical GREEnness (AGREE) (Płotka-Wasylka and Wojnowski, 2021; Pena-Pereira et al., 2020).

Entacapone (99.97%) was generously provided by Al Andalous Co. for Pharmaceutical Industries (6th of October city, Egypt). Clonazepam (99.8%) was supplied by Apex Pharma (Badr city, Egypt). Parkicapone® film coated tablets (200 mg ENT/tablet, B. No. SLHNXG705907) and Amotril® tablets (0.5 mg CLZ/tablet, B. No. 224507) were obtained from a local Pharmacy.

Fresh Dill leaves were obtained from the local market. All reagents and chemicals were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA), including glacial acetic acid, phosphoric acid, thiourea, methanol, and boric acid. Britton-Robinson buffers (0.02 M) with pH ranges from 2 to 12 were prepared. Human plasma samples were collected from Hospitals of Kafrelsheikh University (Kafrelsheikh, Egypt), and they were kept frozen at -20 °C till they were used.

N,S-CDs synthesis was done using a domestic microwave (FMW- 25 KC, 900 Watt, product of Fresh Co., Egypt). Morphology of the developed N,S-CDs was investigated utilizing high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) employed at 160 kV (JEOL, Tokyo). The Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was examined for the developed N,S-CDs utilizing FT-IR Spectrometer (Jasco 6800, Japan). Spectrophotometric measurements were conducted using a double beam spectrophotometer (PG Instrument, UK). Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, United States) was utilized for fluorescence measurements. The equipment was equipped with a Xenon flash lamp, maintained at a voltage of 780 V, and set at a 5 nm slit width. As well, elemental analysis of N,S-CDs was performed using energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy attached to scanning electron microscope (JEOL, JSM-IT100LA, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed on a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-ALPHA XPS instrument (USA) with monochromatic X-ray AL K-alpha radiation -10 to 1350 eV spot size 400 μm at pressure 10-9 mbar with full spectrum pass energy 200 eV and at narrow spectrum 50 eV. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were made on a Shimadzu XRD-6000 X-ray diffractometer (Kratos Analytical, Inc., NY). Vortex mixer (IVM-300p, Taiwan), centrifuge, model 2-16 KL, (Sigma, Germany), Jenway pH meter (Jenway 3510, UK), and ultrasonic bath (SS 101H, USA) were further utilized.

N,S-CDs were synthesized utilizing a microwave-assisted approach, where 3 g of fresh Dill leaves were mixed with 2 g thiourea and charred in a 250 mL beaker in the domestic microwave for 90 s. After cooling at ambient temperature, the product was dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water using sonication for 5 min then it was centrifuged for 10 min at 6000 rpm. After that, filter paper was used to filter the supernatant to remove any insoluble debris then the filtrate was kept at 4 °C until used (Scheme 1 ).

The quantum yield of the so-prepared N,S-CDs was calculated using the following equation (1) (Rurack, 2008):

Where, Φ represents the so-prepared N,S-CDs quantum yield and F is the integrated fluorescence intensity following excitation at 310 nm. Absorbance is represented by A and η is the solvent refractive index which equals 1.33.

2- amino pyridine was used as a reference fluorescent compound after dissolving in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.6 at 310 nm) (Brouwer, 2011) and to reduce the absorption impact, absorbance (Ast and AN,S-CDs) was kept below 0.1.

To prepare standard solutions (1.0 mM), 0.0153 g of ENT and 0.0158 of CLZ were separately dissolved in methanol in a 50 mL volumetric flask. To obtain working solutions within the range of 1.0-15.0 μM and 2.0-25.0 μM for ENT and CLZ, respectively, serial dilutions were carried out with distilled water, and it was found that the prepared solutions were stable when preserved at the refrigerator for at least two weeks.

After optimizing the experimental parameters, 100.0 μL of the so-prepared N,S-CDs were added to aliquot volumes of ENT and CLZ within the range of 1.0-15.0 μM and 2.0-25.0 μM, respectively, in a volumetric flask (10.0 mL). For ENT, fluorescence intensities were measured after adding 1 mL Britton-Robinson buffer of pH 10 and completing the mixture to 10.0 mL with distilled water. While for CLZ, fluorescence intensities were measured after completing the mixture with distilled water to the mark, utilizing 10.0 mL volumetric flasks and without using any buffer. Fluorescence intensities were recorded at 380 nm after being excited at 310 nm, with all measurements being performed at room temperature. Regression equations were derived after plotting the calibration curves using drugs concentrations in μM vs the fluorescence quenching values.

Ten of Parkicapone® tablets (200 mg ENT/tablet) and Amotril® tablets (0.5 mg CLZ/tablet) were weighed and finely pulverized. The amount of powder corresponding to 15.30 mg of ENT and 15.80 mg of CLZ were mixed separately with 25 mL of methanol followed by sonication for 15 min. Then, the mixture was filtered into volumetric flask 50 mL, washed with methanol, and completed to the mark. Subsequent dilution with distilled water was carried out to prepare working solutions using volumetric flasks (10 mL). Then, the procedure outlined under Section “2.6” was followed and the tablets nominal content was measured using the corresponding regression equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for content uniformity testing of CLZ
ce:section.ce:para: In order to determine the tablet content uniformity of CLZ, ten distinct tablets of CLZ were analyzed utilizing the procedure mentioned under Section “ 2.7 ”, following the official USP guidelines (Rockville, 2007).

Into a set of centrifugal tubes, 1 mL aliquots of human plasma were spiked with 1 mL aliquots of ENT standard solutions. The spiked samples were then mixed for 2 min using vortex mixer. After that, 100.0 μL of N,S-CDs and 1 mL of Britton-Robinson buffer with pH 10 were added to each tube. Plasma deproteination was carried out by dilution with methanol to 5 mL. The mixtures were subjected to vortex mixing again for 2 min and centrifuged for 20 min at 8000 rpm followed by supernatant filtration by a 0.45 μm syringe filter. Fluorescence measurements for three concentrations of ENT (1.0, 3.0, and 4.0 μM) were carried out, as illustrated under Section “2.6”, and the corresponding regression equation was obtained.

In this work, an ultrafast, green procedure was developed for the synthesis of N,S-CDs by the microwave-assisted approach in only 90 s, using Dill leaves as a natural source of carbon and thiourea as the doping agent and a source of nitrogen and sulfur (Scheme 1). The produced N,S-CDs displayed bright yellow color under optical light and strong blue fluorescence when exposed to UV light (Fig. 2 ).

Different techniques including FT-IR spectrometry, HR-TEM, UV-visible spectrophotometry, and spectrofluorimetry were utilized for characterizing the so-prepared N,S-CDs. Fig. S1 shows the UV absorption spectrum of N,S-CDs with a distinct UV absorption peak at 243 nm. Fluorescence properties of the produced N,S-CDs were also studied. Fig. 2a displays the fluorescence spectra of the synthesized N,S-CDs, pointing out a strong emission peak at 380 nm following excitation at 310 nm. Emission of N,S-CDs was observed to be dependent on the excitation wavelength (λ ex) range between 300 and 330 nm. It was found that λ ex at 310 nm is the ideal excitation wavelength resulting in the highest emission intensity (Fig. 2b). The developed N,S-CDs also showed a high quantum yield of 24% utilizing 2- amino pyridine as a reference fluorescent compound. The prepared solution of N,S-CDs showed consistent fluorescence intensity for more than 2 months without any obvious precipitation, indicating their high chemical stability and reusability.

HR-TEM was utilized to study the N,S-CDs' surface morphological properties and size, demonstrating that the so-prepared N,S-CDs had a spherical shape and a particle size distribution range from 1 to 5 nm with an average size of 2.7 nm, indicating the successful formation of excellent nanoparticles (Fig. 3 a). The so-prepared N,S-CDs surface functional groups were considered using FT-IR Spectrometer. The FT-IR spectrum demonstrated many stretching peaks including, C-Cl (668 cm-1), CS (1025 cm-1), CC (1637 cm-1), C-N (2061 cm-1), C-H (2950 cm-1), and N-H,O-H (3427 cm-1) (Fig. 3b) (Smith, 2011). The.

XPS was used to analyze the surface functional group composition of the synthesized N,S-CDs. Fig. 4 a shows the XPS spectra of N,S-CDs, which reveal carbon C1s (at 292.97 eV), nitrogen N1s (at 407.33 eV), sulfur S2p (at 170.94 eV), and oxygen O1s (at 540.09 eV). The spectrum of C1s spectrum (Fig. 4b) was deconvoluted into three spectra, peaking at 287.35 (for CO), 294.24 (for C-F3), and 290.82 (for π-π*). The N1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) demonstrates the existence of NO2 (at 405.55 eV) and NN/N–NN (at 403.67 eV). The S2p spectrum (Fig. 4d) demonstrates the existence of C-S-C (at 169.6 eV), R2-SO (at 165.52 eV), and C-SO3-H/C-SO3-Na (at 168.8 eV). The deconvolution of the O1s spectrum (Fig. 4e) showed two peaks at 536.8 for OC–OH and 533.47 eV for C–O–C/C–OH. From the XPS analysis, it can be concluded that our synthesized N,S-CDs are N and S-doped and possibly possess sulfinyl, carboxy, azido, and carbonyl groups. N,S-CDs were also subjected to elemental analysis using EDX to determine their composition and validate the level of nitrogen and sulfur doping. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the prepared N,S-CDs were primarily composed of carbon (59.34%), nitrogen (25.90%), sulfur (6.25%), and oxygen (8.50%). The results demonstrate that a high nitrogen and sulfur doping level was achieved. The XRD analysis was also conducted to determine the nature of N,S-CDs (Fig. 5b). The XRD pattern showed a broad peak at 2θ = 25.98°, revealing the amorphous nature of the prepared N,S-CDs.

In this work, N,S-CDs were eco-friendly synthesized and used as fluorescent nanoprobes for the ENT and CLZ estimation. The addition of increasing concentrations of ENT and CLZ quantitatively quenched the fluorescence intensity of the developed N,S-CDs, as illustrated in Fig. 6 .

Generally, mechanisms of quenching include inner filter effect (IFE), static quenching, and dynamic quenching (Magdy et al., 2021, 2022b, 2022c). IFE is a mechanism of quenching that occurs when the absorption spectrum of the quencher (ENT or CLZ) overlaps with the excitation spectrum of the synthesized N,S-CDs (Zu et al., 2017). As illustrated in Fig. 7 , the excitation spectrum of N,S-CDs greatly overlapped with the absorption spectra of ENT and CLZ, thus, IFE might occur. In order to study more the mechanism of quenching, fluorescence intensity of the prepared N,S-CDs was corrected for any potential IFE following the addition of an increasing concentrations of ENT and CLZ, using equation # (2):

The plot of %E for both observed and corrected N,S-CDs fluorescence intensities versus the concentrations (μM) of the studied drugs (Fig. 8 ) verified that, IFE is crucial in the N,S-CDs' fluorescence quenching by ENT and CLZ.

Other mechanisms like dynamic or static quenching were also investigated using the Stern-Volmer equation (4) as following:
(4)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]
Where: (F0) and (F) represent the fluorescence intensities of N,S-CDs in absence and presence of the studied drugs, respectively, KSV is the Stern-Volmer (S-V) quenching constant, and [Q] is the drug molar concentration (Muttannavar et al., 2018; Magdy et al., 2024).

For studying the mechanisms of quenching that could occur along with IFE, the suggested method was carried out at different temperatures (293, 303, 313 K), as the KSV constant increases by raising the temperature in the dynamic quenching, however in static quenching, it decreases by raising the temperature, and it is not affected by temperature in case of IFE (Magdy et al., 2023c). The KSV values were found to be 60.53 × 103, 60.26 × 103, and 60.79 × 103 L mol-1 for ENT at 293, 303, 313 K, respectively, while for CLZ, the KSV values were 21× 103 L mol-1 at all temperatures, excluding the dynamic and static quenching, and proving that the main mechanism of quenching is IFE (Fig. 9 ).

To study the pH effect on the quenching by ENT and CLZ, Britton-Robinson buffer over the pH range 2-12 was used. Buffer with pH 10 produced the highest increase in the N,S-CDs fluorescence quenching in case of ENT, while for CLZ, no substantial effect on the quenching of N,S-CDs over that range of pH was observed (Fig. S2a).

For ENT, the buffer volume effect was studied over the range of 0.5- 2 mL, observing that, the buffer volume resulting in the highest quenching was 1 mL (Fig. S2b). Therefore, 1 mL of Britton-Robinson buffer of pH 10 was used throughout the study.

The temperature effect was investigated over the range of 20 °C to 60 °C. It was observed that the N,S-CDs quenching by both ENT and CLZ decreased significantly by raising the temperature (Fig. S2c). Consequently, all the spectrofluorimetric measurements were done at room temperature, adding another advantage of the developed method in terms of simplicity.

Time effect on the N,S-CDs fluorescence quenching by each of ENT and CLZ was also investigated at various time intervals (1-60 min). It was observed that N,S-CDs quenching by both drugs was instantaneous and remained stable for at least 60 min (Fig. S2d).
The method proposed was validated according to the guidelines of ICHQ2 (R1) (ICH Guidelines, 2005).

For assessing the linearity range, the concentration (μM) of each of ENT and CLZ was plotted against the fluorescence quenching (F0-F), founding that the linear range was 1.0 - 15.0 μM and 2.0 – 25.0 μM for ENT and CLZ, respectively. Linear regression analysis was applied giving the following equations:
(5)

Where: C is the drugs concentration (μM).
The correlation coefficient (r) was found to equal 0.9999, proving that the suggested approach was sufficiently linear. Results of the analytical validation were summarized in Table 1 .

(7)

Where, Sa and b represent the intercept standard deviation and the slope, respectively. The calculated values of LOD and LOQ indicated the high sensitivity of the suggested approach, which further allowed its application to human plasma samples (
Table 1
).

Accuracy and precision of the suggested approach were studied through comparing the data obtained from the developed method to those from the comparative ones (Koradia et al., 2009; Salem et al., 2004), using the Student t-test and the Variance Ratio F-test, respectively. Table 2 displayed that, in terms of precision and accuracy, there was no major difference between the proposed and comparison methods. Additionally, the developed method intra-day and inter-day precision were investigated, and both drugs demonstrated acceptable %RSD values (less than 1.75 %), as presented in Table S1.

Method robustness was confirmed by the constancy of the N,S-CDs fluorescence quenching by the studied drugs following the small variations in the method's parameters, including N,S-CDs volume (100.0 μL ± 5) in addition to the Britton-Robinson buffer pH (10 ± 0.2) and volume (1 mL ± 0.1) for ENT. The obtained data revealed that, the quenching of N,S-CDs fluorescence by ENT and CLZ was not extensively affected by minor changes in experimental conditions, as presented in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ENT and CLZ in tablets
ce:section.ce:para: ENT and CLZ were successfully analyzed in their pharmaceutical formulations (Parkicapone® film coated tablets and Amotril® tablets) with high %recoveries (98.50-101.31 %) and small %RSD values (≤1.23 %). Table 3 demonstrated that results from the suggested approach and those from the reported methods (Koradia et al., 2009; Salem et al., 2004) agreed well. The statistical examination of the obtained data using the Student t-test and Variance ratio F-test, respectively, revealed that the procedure likewise had good accuracy and precision.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Content uniformity testing of CLZ tablets
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the high sensitivity of the proposed technique and the rapid and accurate measurements of the fluorescence intensity of the individual tablet extract, content uniformity of the potent drug CLZ was efficiently evaluated by following the USP guidelines (Rockville, 2007). As illustrated in Table 4 , the excellent content uniformity of CLZ was demonstrated by observing that the calculated acceptance value (AV) was significantly less than the maximum permitted acceptance value (L1).

Owing to the high sensitivity and selectivity of the suggested approach, it was able to estimate ENT in the spiked human plasma. Plasma concentration of ENT was reported to be 1.2-1.5 μg/mL (4-5 μM) (Belal et al., 2017a). Linear relationship was obtained in the spiked plasma samples upon plotting ENT concentrations (μM) versus the fluorescence quenching (F0-F). Statistical analysis of the results revealed that the mean % recoveries ±SD of ENT in spiked plasma were 100.22 % ± 1.35 (Table 5 , Fig. S4). The following equation (9) represents the linear regression analysis of the data:
(9)
(
F 0

)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Greenness assessment of the developed method
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the greenness and eco-friendliness of the proposed approach, two greenness evaluation tools were utilized including, AGREE (Pena-Pereira et al., 2020) and Complex GAPI (Płotka-Wasylka and Wojnowski, 2021). Complex GAPI is an enhanced tool of GAPI (Płotka-Wasylka, 2018) that evaluates both the processes precede the analytical method and the greenness of the analytical method itself. In Complex GAPI, an extra hexagonal shape was incorporated into the pictogram of GAPI to assess the greenness of the following factors: solvents and reagents, workup and instrumentation, conditions, yield, and purification. On the other hand, AGREE is a metric tool utilized for determining the analytical method's occupational and environmental hazards by evaluating 12 crucial factors. AGREE has a score range from 0 to 1. The more closely a method scores to 1, the greener it is. The proposed method utilized the microwave for the N,S-CDs synthesis using Dill as a natural bio-based source of carbon. It also used buffer and distilled water as a green solvent for the direct determination of ENT and CLZ. It was found that, the method is excellent green with high AGREE score of 0.86 and green colored Complex GAPI, as shown in Fig. 10 .

Unlike the reported chromatographic, electrochemical, and spectroscopic methods, that involve tedious sample preparation, large volumes of organic solvents, and complicated instrumentation, the proposed work provides a simple, rapid, eco-friendly, and cost-effective spectrofluorimetric technique for ENT and CLZ determination. A comparison of the developed N,S-CDs-based probe with the previously reported ones was also carried out (Table 6 ). Regarding the developed probe, as seen in Table 6, the prepared N,S-CDs could determine the ENT and CLZ with high sensitivity and LOD of 0.19 μM and 0.22 μM, respectively, which is 1.5-9 times more sensitive than the previously reported probes (Ahmadi et al., 2019; Min et al., 2021; Rizk et al., 2018; Sachdeva et al., 2021). For the other reported probes (Naghian et al., 2021; Ghafarloo et al., 2020), however, they are fairly sensitive than the synthesized N,S-CDs; they are less green, time-consuming, and require sophisticated synthesis and drastic conditions. Therefore, the developed probe is superior to the previously reported ones for the determination of the studied drugs.

In the present study, an ultrafast and green spectrofluorimetric method for ENT and CLZ determination was developed. The method is based on the microwave synthesis of green N,S-CDs in only 90 s using Dill leaves and thiourea as substrates. The developed N,S-CDs displayed blue fluorescence upon exposure to UV light, and were characterized using HR-TEM, FTIR, spectrofluorimetry, and UV-Vis spectroscopy. EDX, XRD, and XPS were also performed to determine the elemental composition and surface functional groups of the prepared N,S-CDs. The developed method is the first analytical report that describes the N,S-CDs usage for ENT and CLZ determination. The native fluorescence of the produced N,S-CDs was observed to be quenched quantitatively by ENT and CLZ, and the method's validation revealed acceptable % recoveries (98.50-101.31%) and % RSD less than 1.23%. The suggested method revealed high sensitivity and selectivity and was efficiently applied for ENT and CLZ determination in their tablets and human plasma samples. Greenness assessment of the proposed approach and content uniformity testing of CLZ tablets were also studied.
